**His saying, the Exalted:** «*They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also the Messiah, son of Mary*» (9:31). The scribes (الأحبار) is the plural of *ḥabr* (with *fathah* or *kasrah* on the *ḥā*), meaning a scholar, and its usage became predominant for the scholars of the Jews. The monks (الرهبان) is the plural of *rāhib*, meaning one who is clothed in the garment of fear [of God], and it became predominant for the ascetics among the Christians.

Their taking the scribes and monks as lords besides Allah refers to their listening to them and obeying them without restriction or condition; and none is to be obeyed in such a manner except Allah, the Glorified.

As for their taking the Messiah, son of Mary, as a lord besides Allah, it refers to the claim of his divinity in some form, as is well known from the doctrines of the Christians. In the attribution of the Messiah to Mary, there is an allusion to their being unjustified in this taking, as he is a human being, the son of a woman.

Because the two "takings" are different in meaning, He separated them, mentioning their taking the scribes and monks as lords besides Allah first, then conjoining to it His saying: "and also the Messiah, son of Mary" (وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ).

Just as the speech indicates the difference between the two types of lordship, it is also not devoid of an indication that their claim regarding the sonship of Ezra and the sonship of the Messiah is based on two different meanings: an honorific sonship in the case of Ezra, and a sonship of a real nature in the case of the Messiah (عليه السلام). This is because the verse omitted the mention of their taking Ezra as a lord besides Allah, mentioning in its place only their taking the scribes and monks as lords besides Allah.

Thus, he is a lord to them in this sense—either because the honor of sonship necessitates that, or because he is one of their scribes who rendered them a service in renewing their religion that cannot be compared to the service of any other. As for the Messiah, his sonship is other than this sonship.

Regarding His saying: "though they were not commanded except to worship the one God; there is no god except Him" (وَما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلهاً واحِداً لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ), this is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية), meaning: they took for themselves lords while this was the state of affairs.

The speech contains an indication, first: that taking as a lord through obedience is like taking as a lord through worship (العبادة). For obedience, when it is absolute and independent, is worship. The necessary implication of this is that the lord who is obeyed without restriction or condition and in an independent manner is a god (إله), for the god is the object of worship who has the right to be worshipped. All of this is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "though they were not commanded except to worship the one God" (وَما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلهاً واحِداً), where He replaced "Lord" with "God," whereas the apparent context would have suggested saying "though they were not commanded except to take one Lord." Thus, taking for lordship through absolute obedience is worship, and taking the Lord as an object of worship is taking Him as a God; so understand that.

**Second:** That the call to worship Allah alone in His speech—such as His saying, the Exalted: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "there is no god except Me; so worship Me" (Al-Anbiya: 25) and His saying: **فَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ** — "So do not invoke another god besides Allah" (Al-Shu'ara: 213) and the like—just as it is intended to restrict worship in its conventional sense to Him, the Exalted, it is also intended to restrict obedience to Him, the Exalted. This is because He did not hold them to account for their obedience to their scribes and monks except by His saying: "though they were not commanded except to worship the one God; there is no god except Him."

This meaning is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me. That is a straight path’?" (Ya-Sin: 60-61). This is a door from which a thousand doors open.

In His saying: "there is no god except Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ), there is a completion of the word of monotheism (التوحيد) contained in His saying: "though they were not commanded except to worship the one God." For many of the idol-worshippers believed in the existence of many gods, yet they restricted their worship to only one of them. Thus, the worship of one god does not complete monotheism except with the statement that there is no god but Him.

The Exalted has combined the two forms of worship while alluding to the difference between them, and that restricting worship in both its meanings to Him, the Exalted, is the meaning of Islam (submission) to Him, the Glorified, from which there is no escape for the human being. This is found in what He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the call to the People of the Book: **قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims.’" (Al-Imran: 64).

His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: "Immaculate is He from any partners they may ascribe [to Him]!" (سُبْحانَهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ) is a sanctification of Him, the Exalted, from the polytheism (الشرك) contained in their claim of the lordship of the scribes and monks, and their claim of the lordship of the Messiah (عليه السلام).

The verse serves as an explanatory rationale (بيان تعليلي) for His saying at the beginning of the verses: "those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day," for taking a god or gods besides Allah, the Glorified, is incompatible with faith in Allah, nor with faith in a Day wherein there is no sovereignty except for Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths*» (9:32) until the end of the verse. "Extinguishing" (الإطفاء) is the quenching of fire or light. The "with" (بـ) in His saying: "with their mouths" (بِأَفْواهِهِمْ) denotes the instrument or causality.

The mouths are mentioned because the blowing used to quench lights and lamps is done with the mouths. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "This is a marvelous piece of eloquence, alongside its belittling of their status and the weakening of their plot, because the mouth affects weak lights but not great flames." End quote.

It is said in *al-Kashshaf*: "Their state in seeking to nullify the prophethood of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) through denial is like the state of one who wishes to blow upon a great light spread across the horizons—which Allah intends to increase and bring to the ultimate goal of radiance and illumination—in order to extinguish it with a breath and obliterate it." End quote. The verse is an allusion to the state of the Islamic call and what the disbelievers desire for it, and it contains a beautiful promise that Allah will complete His light.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion, that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse.*» (9:33). Guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — is the divine guidance that He coupled with His Messenger so that he might guide by His command. The true religion (دين الحق) is Islam, including the beliefs and laws that accord with true reality.

The meaning is that Allah is He who sent His Messenger—who is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—with guidance—or signs and manifest proofs—and a primordial religion, so that He may make His religion, which is the true religion, prevail over and be victorious over all religions, even if the polytheists (المشركون) should be averse to that.

By this, it becomes clear that the pronoun in His saying: "that He may make it prevail" (لِيُظْهِرَهُ) refers to the true religion, as is understood from the context. It has been said that the pronoun refers to the Messenger, meaning: that He may make His Messenger prevail and teach him all the landmarks of the religion; but this is remote.

In these two verses, there is an incitement of the believers to fight the People of the Book and an indication of its obligation upon them that is not hidden. For they indicate that Allah willed the spread of this religion in the human world, so there must be striving and struggle (المجاهدة) in that. They also indicate that the People of the Book desire to extinguish this light with their mouths, so they must be fought until they perish or are spared through the tribute (الجزية) and humbling (الصغار). Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, refuses but to complete His light and wills to make this religion prevail over others; thus, the turn of fortune, by the will of Allah, is for them against their enemies. So they should not weaken nor grieve, for they are the superior ones if they are believers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*O you who have faith! Indeed many of the scribes and monks consume the people’s wealth wrongfully and bar from the way of Allah.*» (9:34). It appears that the verse is an allusion to some clarification of His saying at the beginning of the verses: "nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion," just as the previous verse was like a clarification of His saying: "those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day."

As for the clarification of His saying: "nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden" through His saying: "Indeed many of the scribes and monks consume the people’s wealth wrongfully," it is a clarification through the most manifest of examples and the most significant in its effect on the corruption of sound human society and the nullification of the purpose of religion.

The Holy Quran enumerates many detailed crimes and sins for the People of the Book, especially for the Jews, in Surah al-Baqarah, al-Nisa, al-Ma'idah, and others. However, financial crimes and transgressions have a status unlike others, especially in this context where the purpose pertains to the People of the Book's corruption of sound human society if they were to have a free hand and their vital independence were established. Nothing corrupts society like financial transgression.

For the most important foundation upon which human society stands is the financial aspect, which Allah has made a means of support for them. Thus, the majority of sins, evils, crimes, transgressions, and injustices end up, upon analysis, either in extreme poverty that calls for the misappropriation of people's wealth through theft, highway robbery, murder, cheating in measure and weight, usurpation, and other financial transgressions; or in extreme wealth that calls for profligacy and extravagance in food, drink, clothing, marriage, and housing, and indulgence in desires and the violation of sanctities, and the extension of dominance over people's wealth, honor, and lives.

All the corruptions arising from both paths end up, upon analysis, in the disorder that occurs in the system governing the acquisition of wealth and the accumulation of riches, and the laws legislated to balance the aspects of ownership that distinguish the rightful consumption of wealth from its wrongful consumption. When this is disordered and souls believe in the possibility of seizing the wealth beneath them and the riches they long for by any means possible, it teaches them to obtain wealth and seize riches by any means—rightful or wrongful—and to pursue every desire of the soul—lawful or unlawful—leading to whatever it may lead. At that point, the calamity occurs with the spread of corruption and the prevalence of moral degradation in society, and the transformation of the human environment into a base animalistic environment where there is no concern except for the belly and what is below it, and where no one's will is governed by policy or upbringing, nor is wisdom understood, nor is an exhortation listened to.

Perhaps this is the reason for the specific mention of consuming wealth wrongfully, especially by the scribes and monks who are responsible for the upbringing of the nation and the reform of society.

Some have counted among their wrongful consumption of people's wealth the money people offer them out of love for them due to their outward display of asceticism and devotion, as well as the consumption of usury (الربا) and ill-gotten gains (السحت), their seizure of the wealth of their opponents, their taking of bribes for judgment, and the issuing and selling of letters of indulgence, and the like.

It appears that what is intended are things like taking bribes for judgment, as preceded in their story in the commentary on His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ لا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ** — "O Apostle! Do not let those who race with each other in faithlessness grieve you..." (Al-Ma'idah: 41) in the fifth volume of this book.

If there were nothing else but what the Church used to do in selling letters of indulgence, it would suffice as a cause for detestation and blame.

As for what was mentioned regarding the offering of wealth to them due to their asceticism, as well as their being specifically assigned endowments, bequests, and general charities, this is not counted as wrongful consumption of wealth. Similarly, regarding the consumption of usury and ill-gotten gains, the Exalted has attributed this in His speech to the generality of their people, as in His saying: **وَأَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَوا وَقَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ** — "and for their taking usury though they were forbidden it" (Al-Nisa: 161), and His saying: **سَمَّاعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ أَكَّالُونَ لِلسُّحْتِ** — "Eavesdroppers for the sake of lies, consumers of the illicit" (Al-Ma'idah: 42). His speech in the verse we are currently discussing concerns what specifically pertains to their scribes and monks of wrongful consumption of wealth, not what is common to them and their general populace.

However, the truth is that the leaders of the religious nation and their mentors in walking the path of servitude—those concerned with the reform of their hearts and actions—if they deviate from the path of truth to the way of falsehood, then everything they have consumed for this purpose and drawn from its benefits is a prohibited ill-gotten gain (سحت) that neither law nor reason makes lawful for them.

As for the clarification of His saying: "nor practise the true religion" through His saying: "and bar from the way of Allah," it is also based on the point we previously mentioned regarding the characterization with the three attributes, the third of which is His saying: "nor practise the true religion." This is an explanation of those of their attributes and actions that corrupt human society and block the path of the just religious government from reaching its goal of reforming the people and forming a living, active society as befits the primordial human being (الإنسان الفطري) directed toward his primordial happiness.

Therefore, He specifically mentioned from the corruptions of their lack of practicing the true religion that which is the pillar of corrupting sound society—namely, their barring from the way of Allah and preventing people from walking it through whatever manifest and hidden ways they are capable of. They have not ceased to persist in this disposition from the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) until today.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*As for those who treasure up gold and silver and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment*» (9:34). Al-Raghib said: "Treasuring (الكنز) is placing wealth upon itself and preserving it. Its origin is from *kanaztu al-tamr* (I packed the dates) in a vessel... and His saying: 'those who treasure up gold and silver' means they store them." End quote.

In the concept of "treasuring" (الكنز) is the preservation of the treasured wealth, its storage, and preventing it from circulating among the people in the various forms of transactions so that it may grow a good growth and its benefit may spread in society—benefiting this one by taking and that one by giving, and another by working with it. It was their custom before the appearance of banks and general storehouses to bury treasures in the earth to hide them from being targeted by ill intent.

The verse, although connected in its verbal arrangement to the preceding verses censuring the People of the Book and rebuking their scribes and monks for their wrongful consumption of people's wealth and barring from the way of Allah, has no evidence from the perspective of the wording that it was revealed specifically about them or is restricted to them at all.

Thus, there is no way to say that the verse was revealed only regarding the People of the Book and prohibited treasuring for them alone, while the Muslims are free to do as they wish with the gold and silver they possess without any blame upon them.

The verse threatens the treasurers with a severe warning and a painful punishment. However, it explains the "treasuring" indicated by His saying: "those who treasure up gold and silver" with His saying: "and do not spend it in the way of Allah." Thus, it indicates that what Allah hates of treasuring is that which is accompanied by the withholding of its spending in the way of Allah when there is a path [necessitating it].

The "way of Allah" (سبيل الله), as inferred from His speech, is that upon which the standing of Allah's religion depends and which ensures its structure does not collapse—such as Jihad and all the interests of the religion that must be preserved, and the affairs of the Muslim society whose social contract would dissolve if they were dissolved, and the obligatory financial rights through which the religion has established the backbone of the religious society. So, whoever treasures gold or silver while the need is present and the necessity is pressing has treasured gold and silver and not spent them in the way of Allah; so let him be informed of a painful punishment, for he has preferred himself over his Lord and prioritized the potential need of himself or his child over the certain need of the religious society.

This is inferred from what is in the following verse: "This is what you treasured for yourselves" (9:35), for it indicates that the rebuke is directed at them because they reserved it for themselves and preferred it—fearing their own need for it—over the way of Allah, through which is the life of human society in this world and the Hereafter. They have also betrayed Allah and His Messenger in this from another perspective, which is the hiding and concealing. For if it were manifest and circulating in hands, it would be possible for the ruler (ولي الأمر) to command its spending for a present religious need. But when it is treasured as a treasure and hidden from sight, it is not noticed, and the necessary need remains standing on one side while the treasured wealth—which is the only means to fulfill the need—remains on the other side, despite the one who treasured it having no immediate need for it.

Thus, the verse only prohibits treasuring because of this characteristic: the treasurer's preference of himself with the wealth without needing it over the way of Allah while the need for it exists. It is enough for you to know that Islam does not limit the principle of ownership from the perspective of quantity; so if this treasurer had multiples of what he treasured of gold and silver and did not store it as a treasure but rather placed it in the position of circulation—benefiting himself by thousands upon thousands and benefiting others through buying, selling, labor, and so on—no religious prohibition would be directed at him. This is because since he placed it before the eyes of the people and ran it in the course of sound and beneficial growth, he did not hide it nor prevent it from being spent in the way of Allah. Even if he did not spend it in the way of Allah [directly], he is in such a state that if the ruler of the Muslims wished, he could command him to spend it in what he sees as a necessary expenditure. Thus, he is not a treacherous wrongdoer if he does not spend while being within the sight and hearing of the ruler.

So the verse is looking at the treasuring that is accompanied by the refusal to spend in the obligatory financial rights—not in the sense of the obligatory alms (الزكاة) only, but in a sense that encompasses it and other things upon which the necessity of the religious society depends, such as Jihad, the preservation of lives from destruction, and the like.

As for the recommended spending (الإنفاق المستحب)—such as providing extra for one's family, and giving and bestowing wealth upon the poor beyond the necessity of their lives—even if it is possible for us to call it "spending in the way of Allah," the very evidence explaining its recommended nature reveals that it is not part of this "spending in the way of Allah" mentioned in this verse. Thus, treasuring wealth and not spending it in a recommended manner when there is no necessary path to spend it in is not part of the treasuring prohibited in this verse. This is what the noble verse indicates. There has been a long dispute among commentators regarding it due to some theological discussions pertaining to it, and we shall provide a discussion on it after concluding the traditional research (البحث الروائي) related to the verses, if Allah wills.

His saying at the end of the verse: "inform them of a painful punishment" (فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ) is a threat of punishment that indicates its severe prohibition.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*on the day when they will be heated in the fire of hell, and their foreheads and their sides and their backs will be branded with them*» (9:35) until the end of the verse. "Heating" (إحماء) a thing is making it hot to the touch. "Heating upon it" (الإحماء عليه) is kindling fire to make it hot, and *iḥmā’* is beyond mere warming. "Branding" (الكي) is the application of a hot object to the body.

The meaning is: that punishment informed of is on a day when fire is kindled upon those treasures in the fire of Hell, so they become heated by the fire and are applied to their foreheads, their sides, and their backs, and it is said to them at that time: "This is what you treasured for yourselves; so taste what you used to treasure!" (هذا ما كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ). Thus, it has returned as a punishment upon you by which you are punished.

Perhaps the specific mention of the foreheads, sides, and backs is because they humbled themselves to [the wealth]—and prostration is with the foreheads; and they sought refuge in it—and refuge is with the sides; and they leaned upon it—and leaning is with the backs. Other reasons have also been suggested, and Allah knows best.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), in the tradition of the swords which he mentioned from his father—he said: "As for the three well-known swords: a sword against the polytheists of the Arabs; Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** — 'slay the polytheists wherever you find them' (9:5)."

He said: "And the second sword is against the protected people (أهل الذمة). Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً** — 'and speak kindly to people' (2:83); this verse was revealed regarding the protected people, then it was abrogated by the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **قاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَلا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ـ وَلا يُحَرِّمُونَ ما حَرَّمَ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ ـ وَلا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ ـ حَتَّى يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَنْ يَدٍ وَهُمْ صاغِرُونَ** — 'Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, after being humbled' (9:29). So whoever among them is in the Abode of Islam (دار الإسلام), nothing shall be accepted from him except the tribute or death; and their wealth is spoils (فيء) and their offspring are captives (سبي). If they accept the tribute upon themselves, it is forbidden for us to take them as captives, and their wealth is forbidden [to us], and marriage with them is lawful for us. And whoever among them is in the Abode of War (دار الحرب), it is lawful for us to take them as captives and their wealth [is lawful], but marriage with them is not lawful; and nothing is accepted except entering the Abode of Islam, or the tribute, or death."

In it, with its chain of narration from Talhah ibn Zayd from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Sunnah has established that the tribute is not taken from the insane (المعتوه) nor from the one whose intellect is overcome."

In it, with its chain of narration from Abu Yahya al-Wasiti from some of our companions, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the Magians: 'Did they have anything?' He replied: 'Yes. Has there not reached you the letter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to the people of Mecca: "Embrace Islam, otherwise I shall declare war against you"? So they wrote to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Take the tribute from us and leave us to the worship of idols." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wrote to them: "I do not take the tribute except from the People of the Book."'"

"So they wrote to him—intending thereby to call him a liar: 'You claimed that you do not take the tribute except from the People of the Book, yet you took the tribute from the Magians of Hajar.' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wrote to them: 'The Magians had a prophet whom they killed and a book which they burned. Their prophet came to them with their book on twelve thousand cowhides.'"

**I say:** Regarding these meanings, there are other narrations preserved in the collections of Hadith, and the full discussion of the issues of tribute (الجزية), land tax (الخراج), and others is in Jurisprudence (الفقه).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Asakir narrated from Abu Umamah from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Fighting is of two types: fighting the polytheists until they believe or pay the tribute out of hand while they are humbled; and fighting the rebellious group (الفئة الباغية) until they return to the command of Allah—and if they return, they are given justice."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Mujahid regarding His saying: "Fight those who do not have faith in Allah..." the verse—he said: "This was revealed when Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his companions were commanded with the expedition of Tabuk."

**I say:** The narrations have already preceded under the verse of Mubahalah (آية المباهلة) that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) affirmed the tribute upon the Christians of Najran, and that was—according to the most sound narrations—in the sixth year of the Hijrah, years before the expedition of Tabuk. Similarly, his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) call to the kings of Rome, Egypt, and Persia—who were from the People of the Book—was in the year six.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from al-Zuhri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) took the tribute from the Magians of the people of Hajar, and from the Jews of Yemen and their Christians—one dinar from every adult."

In it, Malik, al-Shafi'i, Abu Ubayd in *Kitab al-Amwal*, and Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Ja'far from his father that Umar ibn al-Khattab consulted the people regarding the Magians and the tribute. Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say: 'Treat them according to the Sunnah of the People of the Book.'"

In it, Abd al-Razzaq narrated in *al-Musannaf* from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he was asked about taking—

...the *jizyah* (الجزية) — *the protection tax paid by non-Muslims under Islamic rule* — from the Magians. He said: "By Allah, there is no one on earth today more knowledgeable about that than I. The Magians were a People of the Book (أهل كتاب) who recognized it and possessed a knowledge they used to study. Then their leader drank wine and became intoxicated, and he fell upon his sister. A group of Muslims saw him. When morning came, his sister said: 'You have done such-and-such to me, and a group of people saw you who will not keep your secret.' So he summoned the greedy ones and said to them: 'You know that Adam (peace be upon him) used to marry his sons to his daughters.'"

Those who had seen him came and said: "Woe to the one far removed! Indeed, the legal punishment (حد) of Allah is upon your back." So those who were with him killed them. Then a woman came and said to him: "Yes, I indeed saw you." He said to her: "Woe to the prostitute of the children of so-and-so!" She replied: "Indeed, by Allah, she was a prostitute but then she repented," so he killed her. Then, what was in their hearts and their books was taken away by night, and by morning they possessed nothing.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللهِ** "The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah’" [9:30], it is narrated from Atiyyah al-Awfi from Abu Sa’id al-Khudri, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: "Allah’s wrath intensified against the Jews when they said, 'Ezra is the son of Allah'; Allah’s wrath intensified against the Christians when they said, 'The Messiah is the son of Allah'; and Allah’s wrath intensified against whoever shed my blood and harmed me regarding my progeny (عترتي)."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bukhari recorded in his *Tarikh* from Abu Sa’id al-Khudri, who said: "On the day of Uhud, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was wounded in his face and his incisor tooth was broken. On that day, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) stood raising his hands, saying: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, His wrath intensified against the Jews for saying, "Ezra is the son of Allah"; His wrath intensified against the Christians for saying, "The Messiah is the son of Allah"; and indeed, Allah’s wrath has intensified against whoever shed my blood and harmed me regarding my progeny.'"

**I say:** Narrations have been reported in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and elsewhere from Ibn Abbas, Ka'b al-Ahbar, al-Suddi, and others regarding the story of Ezra that are more akin to the *Isra'iliyyat* (الإسرائيليات) — *Judeo-Christian traditions incorporated into Islamic exegesis* — and it appears that all of them trace back to Ka'b.

In *al-Ihtijaj* by al-Tabarsi, narrated from Ali (peace be upon him), he said: **قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ** "May Allah assail them! Where do they stray?" [9:30], meaning: Allah has cursed them. "Where do they stray?" Thus, He named the curse (اللعنة) as "assailing" (قتالاً). Similarly: **قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** "Perish man! How ungrateful is he!" [80:17], meaning: Cursed is man.

**I say:** This has also been narrated through Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas. In any case, it is an interpretation (تفسير) that follows the necessary meaning (المعنى) rather than the literal intent (المراد اللفظي).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "They took their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah" [9:31]. He said: "By Allah, they did not call them to worship (عبادة) themselves; had they called them to worship themselves, they would not have answered them. Rather, they made lawful for them what was forbidden (حراماً) and forbade for them what was lawful (حلالاً), so they worshipped them from where they did not realize."

**I say:** al-Barqi narrated this meaning in *al-Mahasin*, and al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Abu Basir and Jabir, both from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), and from Hudhayfah. It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several chains from Hudhayfah.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "They took their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah," he said: "As for the Messiah, some of them magnified him within themselves until they claimed he was a god and the son of Allah; a group of them said: 'The third of three'; and a group of them said: 'He is Allah.'

As for His saying: **أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ** 'their scribes and their monks,' they obeyed them and took their word, followed what they commanded them, and practiced the religion they called them to. Thus, they took them as lords through their obedience (طاعتهم) to them and their abandonment of the command of Allah, His Books, and His Messengers—casting them behind their backs. Whatever the scribes and monks commanded them, they followed and obeyed, while they disobeyed Allah." (The narration continues).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from *al-Majma'*, he said: al-Tha'labi narrated with his chain from Adi ibn Hatim, who said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) while a gold cross was around my neck. He said to me: 'O Adi, cast off this collar (الربق).'"

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Saduq with his chain to Abu Basir, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِ** "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion" [9:33]: "By Allah, its interpretation (تأويلها) has not yet been realized, nor will its interpretation be realized until the Riser (القائم) — *the divinely guided leader from the progeny of the Prophet who will establish justice* — emerges. When the Riser emerges, there will remain no disbeliever (كافر) in Allah nor any polytheist (مشرك) regarding the Imam except that he will detest his emergence. Even if a disbeliever were inside the belly of a rock, it would say: 'O believer, inside me is a disbeliever, so break me and kill him!'"

**I say:** al-Ayyashi narrated a similar meaning from Abu al-Miqdam from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and from Sama'ah from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him). Likewise, al-Tabarsi narrated the same from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, it is stated that it was revealed concerning the Riser from the Progeny of Muhammad (peace be upon him), and the meaning of its revelation concerning him is that he is its interpretation, as indicated by the narration of al-Saduq.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* recorded from Jabir regarding His saying: **لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ** "that He may make it prevail over all religions" [9:33], he said: "That will not happen until there remains no Jew, nor Christian, nor follower of any creed except Islam; until the sheep is safe from the wolf, the cow from the lion, and the human from the snake; until a mouse does not gnaw a sack; until the *jizyah* is abolished, the cross is broken, and the pig is killed—and that is when Jesus, son of Mary (peace be upon him), descends."

**I say:** The meaning of "abolishing the *jizyah*" is that it will be abandoned because there will be no need for it, as there will be no subject for it, based on the context of the beginning of the tradition. What these narrations indicate regarding the disappearance of disbelief (كفر) and polytheism (شرك) on that day is supported by other narrations. However, there are other narrations indicating that the Mahdi (peace be upon him) will impose the *jizyah* on the People of the Book after his emergence.

This may be supported by the saying of the Exalted regarding the People of the Book: **وَأَلْقَيْنا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** "and We have cast enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection" [5:64], and **فَأَغْرَيْنا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** "so We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection" [5:14], and similar verses. These verses are not devoid of an apparent indication (ظهور) of their remaining until the Day of Resurrection, unless they are a metaphor (كناية) for the eternal removal of affection between them. Some discussion on this meaning has preceded in the commentary on those verses.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* as well, Ibn al-Durays recorded from Ilba' ibn Ahmar that when Uthman ibn Affan intended to write the codices (المصاحف), they wanted to omit the "and" (الواو) that is in *Bara'ah*: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ** "And those who treasure up gold and silver" [9:34]. Ubayy said: "You shall certainly include it, or I shall place my sword upon my shoulder." So they included it.

In *Amali al-Shaykh*, he said: A group informed us from Abu al-Mufaddal, who traced the chain, saying: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: "When this verse was revealed: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ ـ وَلا يُنْفِقُونَها فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ** 'And those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment' [9:34]—everything for which the alms-tax (الزكاة) is paid is not a 'treasure' (كنز), even if it were beneath seven earths; and every wealth for which the alms-tax is not paid is a 'treasure,' even if it were above the ground."

**I say:** A similar meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Adi and al-Khatib from Jabir from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), and likewise through other chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

In the same source, with his chain to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) from his father Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), that he was asked about dinars and dirhams and what is incumbent upon the people. Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "They are the seals (خواتيم) of Allah in His earth; Allah made them for the benefit (مصلحة) of His creation, and through them their affairs and needs are set right. So, whoever is given much of them and fulfills the right of Allah (حق الله) regarding them—paying their alms-tax—that is what he sought and it is purified for him. But whoever is given much of them and is stingy with them, not paying the right of Allah regarding them, and uses them to build structures, then he is the one upon whom the threat of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in His Book is justified. Allah the Exalted says: **يَوْمَ يُحْمى عَلَيْها فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوى بِها جِباهُهُمْ** 'on the day when they will be heated in the fire of Hell, and their foreheads... will be branded with them' [9:35]."

**I say:** The narration supports what we previously derived from the verse.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he said: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari used to go out every day—while he was in Syria—and cry out at the top of his voice: "Give tidings to the hoarders of treasures (أهل الكنوز) of branding (كي) on the foreheads, branding on the flanks, and branding on the backs, until the heat circulates within their insides!"

**I say:** al-Tabarsi in *al-Majma'* derived from the narration the reason for specifying the foreheads (الجباه), flanks (الجنوب), and backs (الظهور) among the human limbs for mention in the verse. He noted that the purpose of punishing them in this manner is to bring the heat of the fire into their insides (أجوافهم), which are inside the heads—so their foreheads are branded—and inside the chests and bellies—so their flanks and backs are branded.

It is possible to complete what he mentioned by saying that they will be thrown down on their faces with their heads inverted, as suggested by the reports and some verses; then their limbs will be branded from above, resulting in the branding of the foreheads, flanks, and backs.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd al-Razzaq recorded in *al-Musannaf* from Abu Dharr, who said: "Give tidings to the hoarders of treasures of branding on the foreheads, the flanks, and the backs."

In the same source, Ibn Sa'd, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Zayd ibn Wahb, who said: I passed by Abu Dharr at al-Rabadhah and said: "What brought you to this land?" He said: "We were in Syria and I read: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ ـ وَلا يُنْفِقُونَها فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ** 'And those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment.' Mu'awiyah said: 'This is not about us; it is about the People of the Book.' I said: 'It is about us and them.'"

In the same source, Muslim and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from al-Ahnaf ibn Qays, who said: Abu Dharr came and said: "Give tidings to the hoarders of branding from the direction of their backs that will come out of their flanks, and branding from their foreheads that will come out of the napes of their necks." I said: "What is this?" He said: "I have said nothing except what I heard from their Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم)."

In the same source, Ahmad recorded in *al-Zuhd* from Abu Bakr al-Munkadir, who said: Habib ibn Salamah—who was the governor of Syria—sent three hundred dinars to Abu Dharr and said: "Use this for your needs." Abu Dharr said: "Return it to him. Did he find no one more deceived regarding Allah than us? We have nothing but the shade we take cover in, three sheep that return to us in the evening, and a female servant of ours who gives us charity through her service. Furthermore, I truly fear the surplus (الفضل)."

In the same source, al-Bukhari and Muslim recorded from al-Ahnaf ibn Qays, who said: I sat with a group from Quraysh, and a man with coarse hair, clothes, and appearance came until he stood over them and gave greetings. Then he said: "Give tidings to the hoarders of heated stones (رضف) that will be heated in the fire of Hell, then placed on the nipple of one of them until it comes out from the bone of his shoulder (نغض كتفه), and placed on the bone of his shoulder until it comes out from his nipple, dangling."

Then he turned away and sat by a pillar. I followed him and sat with him, not knowing who he was. I said: "I do not think the people liked what you said." He said: "They understand nothing. My close friend (خليلي) told me..." I asked: "Who is your close friend?" He said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم). Do you see [Mount] Uhud?" I said: "Yes." He said: "I would not like to have the equivalent of Uhud in gold to spend it all—except for three dinars. Indeed, these people do not understand; they only amass for the world. By Allah, I do not ask them for anything of the world, nor do I seek their legal opinion (أستفتيهم) on religion until I meet Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In *Tarikh al-Tabari*, from Shu'ayb from Sayf from Muhammad ibn Awn from Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas: Abu Dharr entered upon Uthman while Ka'b al-Ahbar was with him. He said to Uthman: "Do not be satisfied with people merely refraining from harm until they offer kindness. It is necessary for the one who pays the alms-tax not to limit himself to it, but to do good to neighbors and brothers and maintain ties of kinship."

Ka'b said: "Whoever has performed the obligatory duty (الفريضة) has fulfilled what is upon him." Abu Dharr raised his staff (محجنه) and struck him, wounding him. Uthman asked him to forgive him, and he did. Uthman said: "O Abu Dharr, be conscious of Allah and restrain your hand and your tongue." Abu Dharr had said to Ka'b: "O son of a Jewish woman, what are you and what is your business here?"

**I say:** The stories of Abu Dharr and his disagreement with Uthman and Mu'awiyah are well-known and recorded in history books. Contemplating the aforementioned traditions—what he said to Mu'awiyah that the verse is not exclusive to the People of the Book, and his address to Uthman and confrontation with Ka'b—indicates that he understood from the verse what we previously presented: that it threatens against refraining from spending in the obligatory way (السبيل الواجب).

This is supported by an analysis of the situation at that time. The people had split into two groups: the masses (عامة) who could not afford their daily sustenance and found nothing to cover their nakedness, having no way to meet their most essential needs; and the elite (خاصة) whom the world had intoxicated with the sum of its wealth and possessions, hoarding hundreds of thousands and millions from the gifts of the caliphate, the spoils of war, and the land tax (الخراج).

It suffices for insight to review what histories have recorded of the wealth of the companions—in cash, slaves, estates, and towering palaces—and what Mu'awiyah and the rest of the Umayyads introduced in Syria and elsewhere of Caesarean and Chosroan styles.

Islam does not accept any of this, nor does it permit this flagrant disparity without the classes being brought closer through spending (الإنفاق), and the general conditions being rectified by the wealthy turning toward the poor, and the strong toward the weak.

It is sometimes said that Abu Dharr, through his own personal reasoning (اجتهاد), believed that wealth exceeding the necessary amount spent to satisfy hunger and cover nakedness was a "treasure" (كنز) that must be spent in the way of Allah, or that he was calling for asceticism (الزهد) in the world.

However, what is found in some of his speech in the narrations refutes this. He did not base anything he said on his own reasoning or personal opinion; rather, he said: "I have said nothing to them except what I heard from their Prophet," and "My close friend said such-and-such." The narration is authentic and widely transmitted (مستفيضة) through the chains of both sects from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) that he said: "The green [sky] has not shaded, nor has the dust [earth] carried, one with a tongue more truthful than Abu Dharr."

Thereby, the corruption of what Shaddad ibn Aws mentioned—in what Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from him—becomes apparent: "Abu Dharr would hear from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), then go out to his desert. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) would grant a concession (رخصة) after that, and Abu Dharr would not hear the concession, so he would hold to the first command he heard before that."

This is because what was mentioned from Abu Dharr was only his saying that the verse of treasure is not exclusive to the People of the Book but encompasses them and the Muslims. This is not an instance of what was mentioned in the narration regarding strictness (العزيمة) and concession. Likewise, his saying that paying the alms-tax alone does not suffice for the permissibility of hoarding and not spending in the obligatory way of Allah—how could it be imagined of him that he would not have heard that spending from it is recommended (مستحب) just as some of it is obligatory (واجب), or that he would not know that the evidences for recommended spending are the best clarifiers of the verse of treasure?

Even weaker than that is what al-Tabari relied upon in his *Tarikh*. He narrated from Shu'ayb from Sayf from Atiyyah from Yazid al-Faq'asi, who said: When Ibn al-Sawda' arrived in Syria, he met Abu Dharr and said: "O Abu Dharr, are you not amazed at Mu'awiyah saying: 'The wealth is the wealth of Allah'? Indeed, everything belongs to Allah! It is as if he wants to keep it for himself away from the Muslims and erase the name of the Muslims."

So Abu Dharr came to him and said: "What calls you to name the wealth of the Muslims 'the wealth of Allah'?" He said: "May Allah have mercy on you, O Abu Dharr. Are we not the servants of Allah, and the wealth is His wealth, the creation is His creation, and the command is His command?" He said: "Do not say it." He replied: "I will not say it does not belong to Allah, but I will say: 'the wealth of the Muslims.'"

He said: Ibn al-Sawda' came to Abu al-Darda', who said to him: "Who are you? By Allah, I think you are a Jew!" Then he came to Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who grabbed him and brought him to Mu'awiyah, saying: "By Allah, this is the one who incited Abu Dharr against you."

Abu Dharr stood in Syria and began saying: "O assembly of the rich and worst of the poor! Give tidings to those who treasure up gold and silver and do not spend it in the way of Allah of a place in the fire wherewith their foreheads, flanks, and backs will be branded." (The narration continues).

The gist of it is that Abu Dharr only initiated what he did and persisted in it due to the prompting of Ibn al-Sawda'. These two from whom the tradition is narrated—and from whom most of the stories of Uthman are narrated, namely Shu'ayb and Sayf—are among the well-known liars and fabricators (الوضاعين) mentioned and criticized by the scholars of biographical evaluation (علماء الرجال).

What they fabricated regarding the story of Ibn al-Sawda'—whom they named Abdullah ibn Saba', and to whom his story traces back—is among the fabricated traditions. Recent researchers have concluded that this Ibn al-Sawda' is one of the mythical fabrications (الموضوعات الخرافية) that has no basis.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Jabir, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: "There is no possessor of a treasure who does not pay its right except that it will be brought on the Day of Resurrection and his brow and forehead will be branded with it, and it will be said to him: 'This is your treasure with which you were stingy.'"

In the same source, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* and Abu Bakr al-Shafi'i in *al-Ghaylaniyyat* recorded from Ali, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: "Indeed, Allah has obligated upon the wealthy Muslims in their wealth the amount that suffices their poor. The poor will never suffer when they are hungry or naked except by what their wealthy withhold. Lo! Indeed, Allah will call them to a severe account or punish them with a painful punishment."

In the same source, al-Hakim recorded—and authenticated it, though al-Dhahabi weakened it—from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from Bilal, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: "O Bilal, meet Allah poor and do not meet Him rich." I said: "How can I do that?" He said: "When you are provided for, do not hide it away, and when you are asked, do not withhold." I said: "How can I do that?" He said: "It is that, otherwise it is the Fire."

## (Commentary on the Meaning of Treasure)

There is no doubt that the society which man has established according to his primary nature (طبعه الأولي) is only sustained by the exchange of wealth and labor. Were it not for that, human society would not survive for the blink of an eye. Man only draws sustenance from his society by securing matters from the primary earthly materials and performing whatever labor he can upon them, then acquiring for himself what he needs and exchanging the surplus for other things he needs from other members of society. For example, the baker takes for himself the bread he subsists on and exchanges the surplus for the garment woven by the weaver, and so on. Thus, the actions of those in society, within the context of their social gathering, are buying, selling, exchange, and compensation.

What results from economic research is that primitive man used to exchange in his transactions substance for substance (العين بالعين) without being aware of more than that. However, the ratios between substances varied for them according to the intensity of need or lack thereof, and the abundance or scarcity of the needed substances. Whenever a substance was more essential to human need or its existence was scarce, the desire to obtain it increased, and its ratio relative to others rose. Whenever it was far from essential need or became common through abundance, souls turned away from it and its ratio relative to others dropped. This is the origin of value (القيمة).

Then they turned to certain substances that were rare in existence and made them a basis for value (أصلاً في القيمة) against which all other financial substances were measured according to their various ratios, such as wheat, eggs, and salt. These became the axis around which market exchanges revolved. This disposition still circulates among some small societies in villages and among nomadic tribes even today.

They continued in this manner until they discovered certain metals like gold, silver, copper, and the like. They made these a basis to which the ratios of all other substances returned in terms of their values, and a single standard against which others were measured. Thus, they became the self-subsisting currencies (النقود), and others were valued by them.

Then the matter led to gold occupying the first rank, followed by silver, and then others. All were minted with royal or state stamps, becoming the dinar, the dirham, the fils, and so on, in a manner whose explanation would be lengthy and outside the purpose of this research.

It was not long before the two currencies (النقدان) became the basis of value; by them everything is valued, and to them is measured whatever wealth or labor a person possesses. In them centers the fulfillment of every vital need, and they are the criterion of wealth and possession. It is as if the spirit of society in its life is attached to them; its affairs are disrupted by the disruption of theirs. If they circulate in the market of transactions, transactions flow with their circulation, and if they stop, transactions stop.

The function assigned to them in human societies—of preserving the values of goods and labor and determining the ratios of one to another—is clarified by the official papers circulating among people today, such as the pound, the dollar, and others, and the bank checks spread about. These represent the values of things without containing an intrinsic substance that has value in itself; they are almost pure, abstract values.

Contemplating the social status of gold and silver as two currencies that preserve values and serve as standards against which goods and wealth are measured—according to the ratios circulating between them—illuminates that they are representatives of the ratios of things to one another. Since they are, by convention, representatives of ratios—or if you wish, the ratios themselves—the ratios are nullified by the nullification of their convention, imprisoned by their imprisonment and the prevention of their circulation, and they stop when they stop.

We witnessed in the last two World Wars what the nullification of the convention of some nations' currencies created—such as the manat in the Tsarist state and the mark in Germany—of calamity, the collapse of wealth, and the disruption of people's lives. The case of hoarding them and preventing their circulation among people is the same.

To this, Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) refers in the previously mentioned narration from *al-Amali*: "Allah made them for the benefit of His creation, and through them their affairs and needs are set right."

From here it becomes clear that hoarding them is a nullification of the values of things and a killing of the potential within the hoarded amount to revive circulating transactions and keep the market in society standing. By the nullification of transactions and the suspension of markets, the life of society is nullified; and in proportion to their stagnation and stoppage, it stops and weakens.

I do not mean by this their storage in warehouses designated for them, for preserving precious wealth and noble goods from loss is among the duties to which human instinct guides and which sound intellect approves. Whenever the forms of currency circulate in the ways of transactions, however that may be, it is acceptable. When they return, it is necessary that they be stored and preserved from loss and the hands of usurpation, theft, embezzlement, and betrayal that threaten them.

Rather, I mean by it hoarding them and setting them apart from circulation in market transactions and rotation for the rectification of any affair of life and the fulfillment of the needs surrounding society—such as satiating the hungry, quenching the thirsty, clothing the naked, the profit of the merchant, the benefit of the worker, the growth of wealth, the treatment of the sick, the ransoming of the captive, the relief of the debtor (غريم), the uncovering of the distressed, the easing of the worried, the answering of the desperate, and the defense of the core of the righteous society and the rectification of what has become corrupt in the social atmosphere.

These are innumerable instances—obligatory, recommended, or permissible—wherein the limit of moderation should not be exceeded toward the two sides of excess and negligence, or stinginess and squandering. Although there is no sin or legal or intellectual crime in omitting the recommended (المندوب) spending, nevertheless, being the cause of the total nullification of recommended acts and maneuvering to remove their subject matter is among the gravest of crimes and sins.

Consider this in the daily life before you—regarding the affairs of housing, marriage, food, drink, and clothing. You will find that abandoning the supererogatory (النفل) recommended acts of life and subsistence, and restricting oneself strictly to the necessary—which is in the position of the religious obligation—causes a disruption in the affair of life that no repairer can mend and no blocker can close the path of corruption therein.

By this explanation, it becomes clear that the saying of the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ وَلا يُنْفِقُونَها فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ** "And those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment" [9:34] is not far from being absolute (مطلقاً), encompassing recommended spending through the consideration (العناية) that has passed. For in the hoarding of wealth, there is a removal of the subject matter of recommended spending, just like obligatory spending, not merely a lack of spending while the subject matter is suitable for it.

By this, the meaning of what Abu Dharr addressed to Uthman ibn Affan when he entered upon him—as passed in the narration of al-Tabari—also becomes clear, where he said to him: "Do not be satisfied with people merely refraining from harm until they offer kindness. It is necessary for the one who pays the alms-tax not to limit himself to it, but to do good to neighbors and brothers and maintain ties of kinship."

His wording is like an explicit statement—or it is explicit—that he does not view every spending of what remains of one's sustenance after the alms-tax as obligatory. He divides spending in the way of Allah into that which is "obligatory" (يجب) and that which is "proper" (ينبغي). However, he objects to the cutting off of the path of spending from other than the direction of the alms-tax and the total closure of the door of good deeds. In that is the nullification of the purpose of legislation and the corruption of the legislated public interest.

He is saying: This is not an autocratic Caesarean or Chosroan government that has no function except to spread security and restrain harm by preventing some people from harming others, while the people are then free in what they do, not prevented from what they desire of action—whether they exceed or fall short, rectify or corrupt, are guided or go astray and wander—and the one entrusted with their government is free in what he does and is not questioned about what he does.

Rather, it is a social religious government that is not satisfied with people merely refraining from harm. Instead, it leads the people in all affairs of their living toward what is good for them and prepares for every class of society—their leader and the led, their head and the subordinate, their master and the servant, their rich and the poor, their strong and the weak—what suffices for the happiness of their lives. Thus, it meets the need of the rich by the assistance of the poor, and the need of the poor by the wealth of the rich. It preserves the status of the strong through respect for the weak, and the life of the weak through the compassion and oversight of the strong. It maintains the authority of the high through the obedience of the low, and the obedience of the low through the equity and justice of the high. All of this is not completed except by spreading charities, opening the door of good deeds, and acting upon the obligations as befits them and the recommended acts as befits them. As for restricting oneself to the obligatory amount and abandoning recommended spending altogether, in that is the destruction of the foundation of religious life, the nullification of the Lawgiver's purpose, and a swift march toward a disrupted system, chaos, and deep-rooted corruption that nothing can rectify. All of that stems from negligence in reviving the purpose of religion and compromising with the wrongdoers. "Unless you do it, there will be discord in the earth and great corruption."

Likewise is the saying of Abu Dharr to Mu'awiyah in the previously mentioned narration of al-Tabari: "What calls you to name the wealth of the Muslims 'the wealth of Allah'?" He said: "May Allah have mercy on you, O Abu Dharr. Are we not the servants of Allah, and the wealth is His wealth, the creation is His creation, and the command is His command?" He said: "Do not say it."

The phrase that Mu'awiyah, his governors, and the Umayyad caliphs after him used to say—though it is a word of truth and has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) and is indicated by the Book of Allah—they were using it to conclude the opposite of what Allah, the Glorified, intends. For the intent behind it is that wealth is not exclusive to anyone by virtue of might, power, or dominance; rather, it belongs to Allah, to be spent in His way according to the avenues of spending He has designated. If it is what an individual has acquired through earning, inheritance, or the like, it has its own ruling (حكمة). If it is what the Islamic government has obtained from spoils, *jizyah*, land tax, charities, or the like, it also has designated avenues of spending in the religion. In none of that is it for the ruler (والي الأمر) to favor himself or any of his household with anything exceeding his necessary sustenance, let alone to hoard treasures, raise palaces, employ chamberlains, and live the life of Caesar and Chosroes.

As for these people, they only said it to repel the people's objection to them regarding the spending of the Muslims' wealth in the way of their desires and its bestowal upon what does not please Allah, and their preventing it from its people and those entitled to it—that the wealth belongs to the Muslims, yet you spend it in other than their way! So they would say: "The wealth is the wealth of Allah and we are His trustees; we do with it what we see fit." Thus, they permitted themselves to play with the wealth of Allah as they wished and concluded from it the correctness of their action with it as they desired, whereas it only concludes the opposite. "The wealth of Allah" and "the wealth of the Muslims" are one in meaning, yet they took them as two meanings, one of which repels the other.

If Mu'awiyah's intent by his saying "The wealth is the wealth of Allah" were the correct meaning, there would have been no meaning for Abu Dharr's departure from him and his calling out among the assembly of people: "Give tidings to the hoarders of branding on the foreheads, branding on the flanks, and branding on the backs."

Furthermore, Mu'awiyah had said to Abu Dharr that he believed the verse of treasure was specific to the People of the Book. Perhaps among the reasons for his suspicion of them was their insistence during the writing of Uthman's codex to delete the "and" (الواو) from His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ** etc., until Ubayy threatened them with death if they did not include the "and," so they included it, as the narration has passed.

So the story in the tradition of al-Tabari from Sayf from Shu'ayb—even if it was framed so as to judge Abu Dharr as having been mistaken in his reasoning, as al-Tabari admitted at the beginning of his speech—nevertheless, the details of the story judge him to have been correct.

In short, the verse indicates the prohibition of hoarding gold and silver whenever there is a way of Allah (سبيل لله) in which it must be spent, and a calling necessity for those entitled to the alms-tax along with the refusal to pay it, and the obligatory defense along with the lack of funding and the cutting off of the path of righteousness and kindness among people.

There is no difference in the attachment of the obligation of spending between manifest wealth circulating in the markets and the treasure buried in the earth, except that the treasure is characterized by something additional: the betrayal of the ruler in hiding the wealth and his deception, as previously mentioned in the preceding commentary.
